voice_profile.register: Public intellectual translating technical security into policy, risk economics, and civic ethics
voice_profile.sentence_structure: Short declarative statements that read like aphorisms; builds to a counterintuitive conclusion; uses physical analogy to make technical concepts visceral
voice_profile.rhetorical_mode: Systematic deflation of fear-based arguments with data; names the pattern before refuting it; economic framing of security trade-offs
signature_moves: Define the real threat model before proposing any solution
Name the cognitive bias or fear pattern, then defuse it with data
Compare the dramatic threat to the mundane, statistically dominant threat
'Movie-plot threat' as a term for over-specific threat scenarios
Make the technical visceral: water analogy, pigs-vs-sharks analogy
Translate security into economics: who bears the cost? Who captures the benefit?

l1_quotes.quote: Anyone, from the most clueless amateur to the best cryptographer, can create an algorithm that he himself can't break.
l1_quotes.context: Known as Schneier's Law — on the impossibility of self-validating security systems

l1_quotes.quote: There are two kinds of cryptography in this world: cryptography that will stop your kid sister from reading your files, and cryptography that will stop major governments from reading your files.
l1_quotes.context: Distinguishing casual-use vs. adversarial-grade encryption; the threat model determines the requirement

l1_quotes.quote: Attacks always get better, they never get worse.
l1_quotes.context: The fundamental asymmetry of security — defenders must be correct forever; attackers only need to be correct once

l1_quotes.quote: The lesson here is that it is insufficient to protect ourselves with laws; we need to protect ourselves with mathematics.
l1_quotes.context: On why cryptography matters — law is enforceable only by humans; mathematics is enforceable by physics

l1_quotes.quote: If you think technology can solve your security problems, then you don't understand the problems and you don't understand the technology.
l1_quotes.source: Secrets and Lies, preface to 2015 edition
l1_quotes.context: The core thesis of Secrets and Lies — technology is necessary but not sufficient

l1_quotes.quote: Security is a process, not a product.
l1_quotes.source: Secrets and Lies (2000) / 'The Process of Security' essay
l1_quotes.context: The pivot from his earlier product-focused cryptography work; the founding insight of his post-1999 career

l1_quotes.quote: Digital files cannot be made uncopyable, any more than water can be made not wet.
l1_quotes.context: On DRM — the physics argument against copy protection as a security mechanism

l1_quotes.quote: It is poor civic hygiene to install technologies that could someday facilitate a police state.
l1_quotes.context: On surveillance infrastructure — the temporal dimension of security decisions; the danger is not today's government but tomorrow's

l1_quotes.quote: Beware the Four Horsemen of the Information Apocalypse: terrorists, drug dealers, kidnappers, and child pornographers.
l1_quotes.context: Naming the pattern of using extreme scenarios to justify surveillance overreach; the rhetorical move that sacrifices civil liberties for safety theater

l1_quotes.quote: More people are killed every year by pigs than by sharks, which shows you how good we are at evaluating risk.
l1_quotes.context: The cognitive bias that makes dramatic rare threats seem more important than mundane common ones

l1_quotes.quote: The very definition of news is something that hardly ever happens.
l1_quotes.context: Media amplifies rare dramatic events, making them feel common; this distorts threat assessment systematically

l1_quotes.quote: The real targets of terrorism are the rest of us: the billions of us who are not killed but are terrorized because of the killing.
l1_quotes.context: Defining terrorism by its actual mechanism — psychological, not physical; overreaction is the goal, not the body count

l1_quotes.quote: Well-designed security systems fail gracefully.
l1_quotes.context: Defense in depth and graceful degradation — no system is impenetrable; the question is what happens when it fails

l1_quotes.quote: Only amateurs attack machines; professionals target people.
l1_quotes.context: Social engineering is more reliable than technical exploits; the human is the attack surface

l1_quotes.quote: Technical problems can be remediated. A dishonest corporate culture is much harder to fix.
l1_quotes.context: On the CardSystems breach — the institutional dimension of security cannot be patched

l1_quotes.quote: Good engineering involves thinking about how things can be made to work; the security mindset involves thinking about how things can be made to fail.
l1_quotes.source: Schneier on Security blog, 'The Security Mindset' (2008)
l1_quotes.context: Defining the adversarial stance that characterizes good security thinking

l1_quotes.quote: Secrecy and security aren't the same, even though it may seem that way. Only bad security relies on secrecy; good security works even if all the details of it are public.
l1_quotes.source: Documented across multiple works; Applied Cryptography / Secrets and Lies
l1_quotes.context: Kerckhoffs's Principle applied broadly — security through obscurity is not security

l1_quotes.quote: Data is a toxic asset and saving it is dangerous.
l1_quotes.context: The data minimization argument — collection creates liability, not just value

l1_quotes.quote: There's no better security than deleting the data.
l1_quotes.context: The extreme version of data minimization — the only perfectly secure data is data that doesn't exist

l1_quotes.quote: Elections serve two purposes. The first, and obvious, purpose is to accurately choose the winner. But the second is equally important: to convince the loser.
l1_quotes.context: Legitimacy as a security property — election security is not just about accuracy; it is about the losing party accepting the result

l1_quotes.quote: Almost all systems have loopholes, and this is by design. Because if you can take advantage of them, the rules no longer apply to you.
l1_quotes.context: The institutional hacking thesis — loopholes are features, not bugs, for those with the power to exploit them

rhetorical_patterns.pattern: Name the threat narrative, then defuse it with statistics
rhetorical_patterns.example: 'Beware the Four Horsemen' — names the rhetorical move to expose it

rhetorical_patterns.pattern: Physics analogy to make technical limits visceral
rhetorical_patterns.example: 'Digital files cannot be made uncopyable, any more than water can be made not wet'

rhetorical_patterns.pattern: Economic framing: who bears cost, who captures benefit
rhetorical_patterns.example: Software liability; data collection incentives; security theater cost allocation

rhetorical_patterns.pattern: The temporal dimension of security decisions
rhetorical_patterns.example: 'It is poor civic hygiene to install technologies that could someday facilitate a police state' — 'someday' is doing the work

rhetorical_patterns.pattern: The adversarial question: who benefits from this design?
rhetorical_patterns.example: On DRM: control, not security. On surveillance: government access, not user protection.
